t: Not fully explained by dementia or another neurocognitive disorder
Evidence — history, examination, laboratory indicate that disturbance is directly due to medical condition, substance intoxication/withdrawal, or multiple etiologies.
NICE CG103 — Delirium: prevention, diagnosis, management (2010, 2019 update).
CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) — gold standard screening.

Medical cause investigation: complete blood count, electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, Mg, P), glucose, liver, kidney, TSH, B12, troponin, ECG, urine analysis and culture, blood culture, chest X-ray, brain MRI/CT, EEG (seizure suspicion), lumbar puncture (encephalitis/meningitis).
Medication list review — anticholinergics, opioids, benzodiazepines (Beers Criteria).
Affective symptoms are dominant; attention is relatively preserved.
Attention is preserved; onset is different.
Thiamine test; classic triad (ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion).
MMSE or MoCA (initial indicators of cognitive function).
Etiological treatment is the first priority
— Infection, metabolic, medication, bed rest context correction.
Environmental modification — sensory (glasses, hearing aid), daylight, nighttime darkness, quiet environment, presence of loved ones.
Bedside care — hydration, nutrition, urination and defecation, movement.
Antipsychotic (haloperidol 0.25–2 mg, quetiapine, risperidone) — only for severe agitation or when the patient poses a danger to self/others
NICE CG103 — antipsychotics not routinely recommended
; evidence base is limited (Burry L. Cochrane 2018).
Benzodiazepine — only in alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal delirium; otherwise
Prevention — ABCDEF bundle in ICU (Awakening, Breathing, Coordination, Delirium monitoring, Early mobility, Family).
NICE CG103 — etiological and environmental approach first-line; antipsychotics limited.
Burry L. Cochrane 2018 — antipsychotics do not reduce delirium duration or mortality.
Confusion Assessment Method (CAM — Confusion Assessment Method) — Inouye (Inouye S.K.)
4 negative aspects: acute onset/fluctuation + inattention + disorganized thinking or altered consciousness.
— Adapted for intensive care; in patients on ventilator.
— пробуждение, пробуждение дыхания, координация, мониторинг делирия, ранняя мобильность, вовлечение семьи.
Hospital Elder Life Program (HELP — Hospital Elder Life Program) — Inouye (Inouye S.K.)
— multicomponent delirium prevention program; evidence base strong.
In survivors — functional decline, long-term cognitive impairment, increased dementia risk.
Myth 1: "Delirium is 'normal for an elderly person'"
Delirium is a clinical emergency with high mortality; it requires active intervention.
Myth 2: "Antipsychotics are a treatment for delirium"
NICE CG103, Burry Cochrane 2018 — antipsychotics do not reduce delirium duration or mortality; only for severe agitation.
Myth 3: "Benzodiazepine is safe for an elderly agitated patient"
; only in the case of alcohol/BZ withdrawal.
Myth 4: "Hypoactive delirium is 'good' and requires no intervention"
hypoactive delirium linked to worse mortality; problem of under-detection.
Myth 5: "Sleeping is best for the patient with delirium"
Daytime activity, presence of close ones, sensory stimulation are recommended; HELP program is evidence-based.
NICE CG103. Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management. 2010, 2019 update.
Inouye S.K. et al. Clarifying confusion: the confusion assessment method. Ann Intern Med 1990;113(12):941–948.
Burry L. et al. Antipsychotics for treatment of delirium in hospitalised non-ICU patients. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2018;(6):CD005594.
Devlin J.W. et al. SCCM PADIS Guidelines. Crit Care Med 2018;46(9):e825–e873.
